text: The surgeon identifies a right lower quadrant Spigelian hernia and attempts to access the preperitoneal plane, manipulating the tissue layers to create space for the repair.
The surgeon demonstrates the width of the rectus muscle, which appears notably wide in this patient.
The video shows a vertical indentation on the right side of the screen, identified as the linea semilunaris.
The surgeon attempts to access the preperitoneal space in the right lower quadrant, focusing on the area surrounding the spigelian hernia.
The surgeon attempts to bring down the thin transverse salus fascia, revealing its delicate structure during the dissection process.
The surgeon attempts to stay within the preperitoneal space, with visible efforts to navigate the anatomical layers, while considering transitioning into the retro-rectus space.
The surgeon opens the posterior rectus sheath, gaining access to the retro-rectus space.
The surgeon is positioned within the retro muscular retro-rectus space, continuing the procedure in this anatomical area.
The surgeon moves laterally within the retro-rectus space, approaching the linea semilunaris. The grasper visibly contacts a firm boundary, likely the lateral edge of the rectus sheath.
The surgeon pauses dissection to avoid disrupting the linea semilunaris, indicating a careful approach to maintain the integrity of this anatomical structure.
The surgeon performs additional dissection within the retro-rectus space, preparing to continue lateral dissection while maintaining careful technique.

In the retro-rectus space, the surgeon manipulates the tissue so that the fat is elevated and remains against the rectus muscle, ensuring protection of the inferior gastric vessels and neurovascular bundles.
The surgeon continues the dissection in the retro-muscular retro-rectus space, carefully navigating around the linea semilunaris.
The surgeon continues dissection in the retro-rectus space, where the tissue separation is visibly easier compared to the preperitoneal plane.
The surgeon demonstrates the option to either remain within the retro-rectus space, which is easily accessible, or attempt to separate the tissue layers outside of this space.
The surgeon demonstrates the anatomical layers outside the retro-rectus space, highlighting the difficulty of accessing the true preperitoneal layer.
The surgeon navigates between the layers of the attenuated posterior rectus sheath, which are sometimes easier to access during dissection.
The surgeon operates within the retro-rectus space, with the attenuated posterior rectus sheath visible on the floor of the surgical field.
The surgeon divides the attenuated posterior rectus sheath, also referred to as the transverse salus fascia, while progressing laterally in a manner similar to an inguinal hernia repair.
The surgeon exposes the linea semilunaris, a visible anatomical structure forming a lateral boundary in the abdominal wall.
The surgeon continues lateral dissection to create more space for mesh overlap, dividing the posterior rectus sheath visible on the surgical field.
The surgeon cuts the posterior rectus sheath medial to the linea semilunaris, performing a technique similar to a transversus abdominis release (TAR).
Below the arcuate line, the surgeon exposes a thickened layer identified as the attenuated posterior rectus sheath.
The surgeon moves cephalad, creating a pocket behind the posterior rectus sheath to push off the peritoneum, similar to a robotic TAR procedure for a large incisional hernia.
The surgeon pushes the peritoneum aside, facilitating the division of the posterior rectus sheath. The attenuated posterior rectus sheath is visibly being cut.
The surgeon reveals two distinct layers below the arcuate line, similar to the anatomical structure observed above the arcuate line.
The surgeon approaches the spigot line sac and cuts through the attenuated posterior rectus sheath, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon exposes the two layers consisting of the posterior lamella, internal oblique, and transverse abdominis muscles, visible beneath the attenuated posterior rectus sheath.
The surgeon carefully dissects around the hernia sac, ensuring clear separation from surrounding tissues.

The surgeon uses scissors to navigate between layers, moving preperitoneally when the scissors are under the layer and retrorectus when above it, demonstrating the spatial relationship between these anatomical planes.

The surgeon advances further into the surgical field, where the layers of fascia between the retrorectus space and the preperitoneal space visually merge, with the fascia appearing almost non-existent.
The surgeon continues to divide the posterior rectus sheath, carefully extending the incision along the previously identified line.
The surgeon divides the posterior rectus sheath vertically, joining the medial retro-rectus space with the lateral pre-peritoneal space.
The surgeon carefully divides the posterior rectus sheath, ensuring not to cut through the underlying peritoneum, while maneuvering around the hernia sac.

The surgeon uses a grasper to carefully peel the peritoneum away from the posterior rectus sheath, facilitating the division of the sheath. The peritoneum is visibly separated from the underlying tissue.
The surgeon carefully dissects the preperitoneal space, separating the tissue layers to expand the surgical field.
The surgeon continues to peel the peritoneum off the abdominal wall, maintaining the dissection along the preperitoneal space.
The surgeon observes a small remainder of the posterior rectus sheath, noting that the recent dissection did not disrupt the linea semilunaris.
The surgeon makes a vertical incision in the posterior rectus sheath just medial to the linea semilinaris, transitioning from the retro rectus space to the preperitoneal space.
The surgeon navigates from the retrorectal space into the preperitoneal space, carefully respecting the linea alba and linea semilunaris, to develop a large pocket within the preperitoneal space.
